content:
I got slightly tripped up by the k=0 edge case; if I was playing in Fastest mode, I might submit my code before all the test cases finish executing, which might cause me to not 100% the clash.

I wonder if this is an intentional design of the clash? I would add a test case earlier on to check for the k=0 case so that players get to debug slightly faster. But I guess forcing players to read the entire problem statement is also fair :)

Input
* Please rewrite the final paragraph as follows, as the current wording may be unclear:

Given a string [[s]], insert a {{space}} character immediately after every [[k]] <<consecutive identical>> digits to split the repeated sequence. If [[k]] is {{0}}, output [[s]] unchanged.

Output
* Please rewrite the section as follows, as the current wording may cause confusion:

<<Line 1:>> The processed version of [[s]], where {{space}} characters may be inserted between digits.

Cases
* East test and its validator should cover the same scenario but with slightly different inputs and outputs. With this principle in mind, please review the following points when updating the cases.

* Please swap Validator 1 and Test 2.

* Validator 3: Please modify this case so that it more closely matches the scenario covered by Test 3.

* Test 5: Please modify this case so that it more closely matches the scenario covered by Validator 5.

* Long input with head=0 (Test 6): Please create a new validator of [[k]] = 0 to accompany this test.

* Long input with head=1 (Validator 6): Please create a new test of [[k]] = 1 to accompany this validator.

* Long input with head=4 (Test 8) and Long input with head=67 (Validator 8): Please update the value of [[k]] in both cases to a value between 10 and 20, as this range is not currently covered by any existing cases. Also [[k]] = 67 exceeds the upper bound (32) specified in the Constraints section.

Thanks.

Hi,
Thanks for your feedback.
I addressed the points you raised, it should be better now.

My attempt at Shortest mode:

I=input
s=I();k=int(I());r=""
for c in s:r+=" "*((" "*(k+1)+r)[-k:]==c*k)+c
I(r)

Indeed, I hoped the 'TLDR' the last line of the statement was more eye-catching, the statement being already short but I added the check at test case 5, not too early to not bother 'reverse', and hopefully early enough for the racers not to skip it, let me know.
Nice, it seems you can even do without the '*(k+1)' ?
Maybe you could bold "output s unchanged" to make the last line more eye-catching? I guess there's not much else you can do if players don't read the problem statement in full though :)
Ooh you're right about the *(k+1) omission, nice!
Updated with your bold suggestion, thanks for the feedback.

i was able to find a slightly shorter solution:
```
s,k=open(0);k=int(k);r=""
for c in s[:-1]:r+=" "*(r[-k:]==c*k>"")+c
print(r)
```

Yes, it's better now. Some minor issues:

Cases
* Validator 5: Please remove the trailing newline from the input.

* Please renumber the cases as some numbers are missing.

Thanks.
